summary: Gruber investigates the iOS 'Erase Data' feature that wipes an iPhone after 10 failed passcode attempts, a setting he and most people avoid out of fear. A Twitter poll reveals two-thirds of respondents don't use it, mostly fearing children or pranksters could trigger it accidentally. Upon researching the feature's actual implementation, Gruber discovers that escalating timeouts between failed attempts make accidental erasure nearly impossible, requiring over an hour and a half to even reach 10 failed attempts. He concludes the feature is far more cleverly designed than most people realize and enables it himself.
key_points: Two-thirds of poll respondents don't use iOS's 'Erase Data' feature, primarily fearing accidental triggering by children or others
iOS implements escalating timeouts after failed passcode attempts — 1 minute, 5 minutes, 15 minutes, and up to 60 minutes — making it take over 90 minutes to reach 10 failures
The gap between perceived risk and actual risk of this feature is large, causing widespread underuse of a valuable security measure
Apple's design of the feature is quietly thoughtful, protecting against accidental erasure without requiring users to understand the safeguards
money_quotes: I've long been intrigued by this setting, but never turned it on, out of the vague fear that something could happen and I'd wind up with a wiped iPhone.

Among those who don't, the most common response, by far, is that they don't use it because they're the parents of young children, and they fear that their kids will trigger the erasure of their phone.
It turns out this feature is far more clever than I realized, and it's highly unlikely that your kids or jackass drinking buddies could ever trigger it.
These timeouts escalate such that it would take over 3 hours to enter 10 incorrect passcodes.
It seems pretty clear from the responses to my poll that I wasn't alone in thinking that this feature was more dangerous than it really is.
I've got it turned on now, and I can't think of a good reason why anyone wouldn't enable this.
key_insight: Apple's iOS erase-data feature is far better designed than most users realize, with escalating timeouts that make accidental triggering nearly impossible — a case where good engineering is undermined by poor discoverability of its own safeguards.
